text: The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the middle colic vessels, marking a ten-centimeter margin along the bowel wall to ensure good blood supply. The same marking technique is applied over the ligament of Treitz up to the planned colon margin.

The surgeon marks the distal colon margin approximately ten centimeters from the target area for visual reference. They proceed to open the peritoneum over the middle colic pedicle, elevating the tissue for better access. A window is created to the right of the middle colic vessels, revealing the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). The surgeon identifies the pulsations in the tissue and opens over the SMV as it enters the middle colic pedicle.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery and vein, creating a window around these vessels. The tension is relieved before proceeding to ligate them.

The surgeon accesses the lesser sac and carefully dissects tissue from the base of the pancreas. The stomach is visible nearby, illustrating the proximity during this dissection. The surgeon follows the mesenteric root up to the scored mesentery and out to the distal colon margin.

The surgeon divides the omentum at the planned proximal and distal margins, splitting it vertically. They then enter the lesser sac through the gastrocolic omentum to detach residual attachments to the transverse colon.

The surgeon addresses tension created by flexures pulling the colon margins together for anastomosis, removing adhesions between the hepatic flexure and gallbladder. The distal colon is divided, with ICG used to confirm well-perfused colon margins. The cauterized bowel margin is visible, indicating the initial location before ICG revealed a more proximal margin.

The surgeon places the specimen in the pelvis and examines the dissection site, aligning the two ends of the colon with a 3-0 silk suture. The surgeon then proceeds to perform a sewn end-to-end anastomosis, joining the two ends of the colon together.
The surgeon begins a standard anastomosis by sewing together the two staple lines of the bowel using a two OPDS suture, following the completion of previous dissection steps.
The surgeon ties the threostase suture after creating colotomies, checking ICG for perfusion. The common channel is sutured closed in a familiar fashion.
The surgeon brings two sutures towards the middle to tie them together, then sews the last anterior outer layer using a 2-0 PDS suture.
